hypotheses.claim: The extremely high hapax ratio (70.1%) is produced by a systematic null-suffix appended to a smaller core vocabulary: each base word receives one of a small set of suffixes (e.g., -y, -in, -ain, -dy, -edy) to produce surface tokens, meaning the effective vocabulary after stripping common suffixes would shrink to roughly 2,000â€“2,500 unique roots with a Zipf exponent closer to 1.0.
evidence_for: Top words 'daiin', 'aiin', 'chedy', 'shedy', 'chol', 'chey' share recurring suffix clusters (-ain, -edy, -ol, -ey)
Word-final glyph distribution strongly concentrated on y, n, l, r, o â€” consistent with a small suffix inventory
Zipf exponent 0.8946 is flatter than natural language (~1.0), consistent with vocabulary inflation by suffix variation
High hapax ratio is more naturally explained by combinatorial suffix attachment than by rich natural-language vocabulary
evidence_against: If suffixes were purely null/decorative, word-internal glyph entropy should drop after stripping â€” not yet verified
Currier A/B split may reflect suffix-set differences, complicating a uniform stripping rule
Some hapax forms may be genuine rare words rather than suffix variants
hypotheses.test: Enumerate candidate suffix families by clustering word-final bigrams and trigrams; strip the top 10 suffix patterns from all tokens; recompute vocabulary size, hapax ratio, and Zipf exponent on the stemmed corpus.
hypotheses.test_metric: Post-stripping hapax ratio and Zipf exponent
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirm if post-stripping hapax ratio falls below 40% and Zipf exponent rises above 0.95; deny if hapax ratio remains above 60% or Zipf exponent does not change significantly.
tests_remaining: Enumerate candidate suffix families by clustering word-final bigrams and trigrams; strip the top 10 suffix patterns from all tokens; recompute vocabulary size, hapax ratio, and Zipf exponent on the stemmed corpus.
hypotheses.reasoning: If a handful of suffixes account for the vocabulary explosion, stripping them would reveal a compact core vocabulary compatible with a known medieval language, dramatically narrowing the decipherment search space.

hypotheses.claim: The Currier A/B scribal split encodes the same underlying language but with two distinct homophonic substitution tables of different sizes: Currier A uses a smaller homophone set (lower token count, 11,022 words) with higher per-glyph entropy, while Currier B uses a larger homophone set producing lower per-glyph entropy, such that the weighted average glyph entropy of A-folios exceeds that of B-folios by at least 0.05 bits.
evidence_for: Currier B is more than twice as large as A (23,766 vs 11,022 words), consistent with a richer encoding alphabet
Overall glyph entropy 3.8627 bits is a blend; section-level entropy variation (3.71â€“3.90) is consistent with variable homophone table sizes
High-probability belief [0.677] already supports same underlying language for A and B
evidence_against: Entropy difference between sections may reflect content type (label-heavy zodiac vs. prose recipes) rather than table size
A larger homophone set in B would predict lower hapax ratio in B-sections if homophones map to fewer roots â€” needs verification
No direct folio-level entropy breakdown for A vs B has been provided
hypotheses.test: Partition the corpus into Currier-A and Currier-B folios; compute per-folio glyph entropy for each partition; run a two-sample t-test on the per-folio entropy distributions of A vs. B.
hypotheses.test_metric: Mean glyph entropy of Currier-A folios minus mean glyph entropy of Currier-B folios
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirm if A-entropy exceeds B-entropy by â‰¥0.05 bits with p<0.05; deny if difference is <0.02 bits or B-entropy is higher.
tests_remaining: Partition the corpus into Currier-A and Currier-B folios; compute per-folio glyph entropy for each partition; run a two-sample t-test on the per-folio entropy distributions of A vs. B.
hypotheses.reasoning: Detecting a systematic entropy asymmetry between scribal hands would constrain whether the A/B split is a language boundary or a cipher-table boundary, eliminating one of the two leading explanations.

hypotheses.claim: The zodiac section's anomalously low entropy (3.7149 bits, lowest of all sections) is caused by a label-encoding convention in which each label token is drawn from a closed vocabulary of fewer than 150 distinct words, and the type-token ratio for zodiac-section words is significantly lower than for any prose section (herbal, recipes, text-only).
evidence_for: Zodiac section has only 1,297 words across 12 folios â€” consistent with repetitive labeling
Low entropy is most parsimoniously explained by a small, frequently recycled label set
Astronomical section (872 words, entropy 3.7471) shows a similar pattern, suggesting label-driven encoding across diagram sections
High-probability beliefs [0.645, 0.478] already support label-oriented encoding in zodiac
evidence_against: Low entropy could also result from a polyalphabetic cipher that cycles with short period in label contexts
If labels were drawn from a truly closed vocabulary, unique word count for zodiac should be well under 150 â€” this can be falsified directly
The zodiac section could simply contain shorter words, which mechanically lowers entropy
hypotheses.test: Count distinct word types in the zodiac section; compute type-token ratio (TTR) for zodiac vs. herbal and recipes sections; compare average word length distributions across sections using a KS test.
hypotheses.test_metric: Zodiac TTR, zodiac unique word count, and KS statistic comparing zodiac vs. herbal word-length distributions
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirm if zodiac unique word count <150 and zodiac TTR is at least 0.15 lower than herbal TTR; deny if zodiac unique word count >250 or TTR difference <0.05.
tests_remaining: Count distinct word types in the zodiac section; compute type-token ratio (TTR) for zodiac vs. herbal and recipes sections; compare average word length distributions across sections using a KS test.
hypotheses.reasoning: A confirmed label-vocabulary ceiling would let researchers treat zodiac tokens as a constrained ciphertext with known plaintext candidates (month names, star names, nymph labels), creating a potential crib for the broader cipher.

hypotheses.claim: The text-only section's elevated entropy (3.9016 bits, highest of all sections) reflects unenciphered or lightly enciphered natural-language prose, and its word bigram entropy is significantly higher than that of the zodiac and astronomical sections, consistent with natural syntactic variation rather than label repetition.
evidence_for: Text-only entropy 3.9016 bits approaches Latin (~4.0) and Italian (~3.95) glyph entropy, consistent with minimal or no transposition
High-probability belief [0.677] already supports text-only section as closest to natural language
7 folios of continuous prose with no illustrations would naturally have richer syntactic context than diagram labels
evidence_against: Higher entropy could reflect a more complex cipher layer rather than less encoding
Small section size (7 folios, 2,349 words) limits statistical power for bigram entropy estimation
If the text-only section uses a different cipher key, elevated entropy could be artifactual
hypotheses.test: Compute word-level bigram entropy H(w2|w1) for text-only, zodiac, astronomical, and herbal sections; compare section-level bigram entropies using bootstrap confidence intervals; additionally compute the ratio of word bigram entropy to unigram entropy as a syntactic regularity index.
hypotheses.test_metric: Word bigram entropy per section and bigram/unigram entropy ratio
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirm if text-only bigram entropy exceeds zodiac bigram entropy by â‰¥0.3 bits and the bigram/unigram ratio for text-only is closest to values observed in naturally occurring medieval Latin or Italian; deny if text-only bigram entropy is not the highest among all sections.
tests_remaining: Compute word-level bigram entropy H(w2|w1) for text-only, zodiac, astronomical, and herbal sections; compare section-level bigram entropies using bootstrap confidence intervals; additionally compute the ratio of word bigram entropy to unigram entropy as a syntactic regularity index.
hypotheses.reasoning: If bigram entropy in text-only exceeds that of label-heavy sections by a measurable margin, it would suggest the section carries genuine syntactic structure, making it the highest-priority target for plaintext attack and potential crib identification.

hypotheses.claim: Word-initial glyph constraints (o, c, q, s, d dominating) and word-final glyph constraints (y, n, l, r, o dominating) are not merely phonotactic but reflect a codebook structure in which word-initial glyphs encode semantic category (e.g., plant part, action, quantity) and word-final glyphs encode grammatical role (e.g., noun, verb, adjective), such that co-occurrence of specific initial+final glyph pairs is non-uniform and significantly exceeds chance across all sections.
evidence_for: Positional glyph constraints are well-established and too strong to be purely phonotactic in a 25-glyph alphabet
Top words cluster around specific initial-final pairs: daiin (dâ€¦n), chedy (câ€¦y), shedy (sâ€¦y), chol (câ€¦l), chey (câ€¦y) â€” suggesting paradigmatic substitution
If initial glyphs encode semantic class, section-specific vocabulary (herbal vs. zodiac) should show different initial-glyph frequency profiles
evidence_against: A simpler explanation is that initial/final constraints are cipher artifacts (e.g., nulls or padding) with no semantic content
The low glyph alphabet size (25) limits the number of distinct categories encodable at word boundaries
Cross-section initial-glyph frequency data is not yet available to confirm section-specific profiles
hypotheses.test: Build a contingency table of word-initial Ã— word-final glyph pairs across all tokens; compute chi-squared statistic for independence; then stratify by section and compute per-section initial-glyph frequency profiles, testing whether section membership predicts initial-glyph distribution using a multinomial logistic regression or permutation test.
hypotheses.test_metric: Chi-squared statistic for initialÃ—final glyph independence and between-section initial-glyph KL divergence
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirm if chi-squared p<0.001 for non-independence of initialÃ—final pairs AND KL divergence between herbal and zodiac initial-glyph distributions exceeds 0.10 nats; deny if chi-squared p>0.05 or KL divergence <0.03 nats.
tests_remaining: Build a contingency table of word-initial Ã— word-final glyph pairs across all tokens; compute chi-squared statistic for independence; then stratify by section and compute per-section initial-glyph frequency profiles, testing whether section membership predicts initial-glyph distribution using a multinomial logistic regression or permutation test.
hypotheses.reasoning: Non-random co-occurrence of boundary glyphs combined with section-specific initial profiles would provide strong evidence for a structured codebook rather than a simple substitution cipher, fundamentally changing the decipherment strategy required.
